region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent (Punjab region); served in Turkish-Lebanese restaurant context in Singapore

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to tandoori-marinated grilled chicken exists; A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: A full tandoori chicken serving at a restaurant typically comprises one whole bird weighing approximately 800–1000 g after marination and grilling, commonly shared between two to four diners.

nutrient_source.title: Tandoori Chicken, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports tandoori chicken per 100 g edible portion. Values for a full restaurant-portion bird are scaled from this basis. The 'full' designation refers to serving size, not a distinct preparation; per-100g nutrient profile is directly applicable. Minor upward adjustment to sodium reflects restaurant-level seasoning typical of commercial preparation, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 171477) for fat and energy plausibility check.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori chicken as a composite dish. Grilled/roasted chicken is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate food (2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g). Per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021), foods with negligible glycemic carbohydrate content are assigned a conventional GI of 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and classified as 'low' GI. Glycemic load per 900 g serving = (1 × 22.5) / 100 = 0.225.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from marinade and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A full tandoori chicken serving can contribute approximately 4,320 mg sodium, substantially exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-salt preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tandoori chicken contains approximately 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g with a glycemic index of effectively zero, making it a suitable high-protein, low-glycemic option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium and portion size are monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium load at full-serving quantities
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While tandoori chicken is lower in saturated fat than fried chicken preparations, a full-bird serving delivers approximately 20 g saturated fat and high sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to a 150–200 g portion and pair with vegetables rather than refined carbohydrates.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Chicken (Full) is a whole chicken marinated in a spiced yoghurt mixture — typically incorporating garlic, ginger, lemon juice, cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli powder, and garam masala — and then cooked at high heat in a traditional tandoor clay oven. The result is a dish with a characteristic smoky, charred exterior, vivid red-orange colour from the spice blend, and juicy, tender interior meat. Originating in the Punjab region of the Indian subcontinent, tandoori chicken is one of the most internationally recognised Indian dishes and is widely served across South Asian, Middle Eastern, and Turkish-Lebanese restaurants globally, including in Singapore. A full serving refers to one whole bird, typically weighing 800–1000 g cooked, and is commonly shared between two to four diners. Nutritionally, tandoori chicken is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with very low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic-friendly dietary patterns. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 185 kcal, 25 g protein, 8.5 g fat, and only 2.5 g carbohydrate. The primary clinical concern at full-serving quantities is sodium, which can be substantial due to the marinade and seasoning. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal when prepared with compliant ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a full tandoori chicken?
Is tandoori chicken good for diabetics?
Is tandoori chicken high in protein?
What is tandoori chicken made of?
Can I eat tandoori chicken if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), which is an approved primary source for Indian dishes and includes tandoori chicken as a named entry. Plausibility was cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central roasted chicken data. The 'full' designation is a serving-size descriptor, not a distinct preparation variant, so the per-100g profile is directly applicable. Confidence is medium rather than high because the restaurant context (Sultan Palace, Singapore) may involve proprietary marinade formulations that differ from the ICMR-NIN reference preparation, and no direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant product is available.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food entry. 2. Glycemic index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains very low glycemic carbohydrates (2.5 g per 100 g); gi_category low per convention, as per University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Glycemic load per 900 g serving is 0.225. 3. Sodium value of 480 mg per 100 g reflects commercial restaurant-level seasoning; home-prepared versions may be lower. 4. The food is listed under Turkish cuisine in the field survey source due to the restaurant context (Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine), but tandoori chicken is culinarily Indian in origin; cuisine field reflects both contexts. 5. The 'Full' in the food name refers to a whole-bird serving size as opposed to a half-bird portion, not a distinct preparation method. 6. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g, reflecting naturally occurring trans fats from the animal source (vaccenic acid), not industrial trans fats. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA cross-reference; vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as not reported in the source database.
